title: How to Stop Fixing Everyone Around You
meta_description: Discover why you feel compelled to fix everyone around you and how to stop. Your sensitivity is a gift not a burden. Learn to sense without saving.
og_title: How to Stop Fixing Everyone Around You
og_description: Discover why you feel compelled to fix everyone around you and how to stop. Your sensitivity is a gift not a burden. Learn to sense without saving.
body:
You Can't Save Them. Here's Why You Keep Trying.

Let me guess. You walk into a room and you can feel it. The tension between two people who aren't speaking. The sadness someone's trying to hide behind a smile. The quiet desperation of the friend who keeps saying "I'm fine" when they're clearly not. And something in you lights up. Not in a good way. It's more like an alarm going off. You have to fix it. You have to make it better. You have to save them.

I know this because I've been there. Hell, I still catch myself there sometimes. You're not broken for feeling this way. You're not too much. Your nervous system isn't defective. It's actually working exactly as it should - for someone who's calibrated to sense a wider range of emotional information than most people. The problem isn't that you feel too much. The problem is what you do with what you feel.

So let's talk about how to stop fixing everyone around you. Not because you don't care. Not because you're selfish. But because your fixing isn't helping anyone - least of all you.

The Bright Wound: Why You Became The Fixer

Here's the part nobody tells you about being highly sensitive or empathic. You didn't just wake up one day with this urge to fix everyone. You were trained for it. Conditioned. Probably before you could even talk.

Think back. When you were a kid, did you learn that your needs didn't matter as much as keeping the peace? Did you figure out early that if you could just make mom happy, or calm dad down, or stop your sister from crying, things would be okay? Did you learn that your value was tied to how much you could give, how much you could smooth over, how much you could fix?

That's the bright wound. It's the scar that looks like a gift but acts like a trap. You're so good at sensing what others need that you forgot you have needs too. You're so good at carrying other people's weight that you forgot you have your own load to carry. You're so good at fixing that you never learned how to just be.

Does that land? Because if it does, you're not alone. This is the story of almost every empath I've ever worked with. And the first step to stopping the fixing is understanding where it comes from.

The Cost of Being Everyone's Therapist

Let me be real with you. Fixing everyone around you is expensive. Not in money - though you've probably spent plenty on coffees for friends who never return the favor. I'm talking about the real cost. The one you can't get back.

"Every time you carry someone else's weight, you lay down your own. The pile gets smaller for them and bigger for you. That's not love. That's self-abandonment dressed up as service."

Here's what it's costing you:

  • Your energy. You're exhausted all the time. Not because you're lazy, but because you're running on empty. You're giving from a well that never gets refilled.
  • Your relationships. The people you fix don't actually get better. They get dependent. They learn that you'll show up to save them, so they never learn to save themselves. And you? You start to resent them for needing you.
  • Your own growth. While you're busy fixing everyone else, who's fixing you? Nobody. Because you've made it clear you don't need help. You're the strong one. The capable one. The one who has it all together. But that's a lie, isn't it?
  • Your intuition. This is the cruelest one. The more you focus on other people's problems, the more you drown out your own inner voice. You lose access to the one thing that could actually guide you - your own knowing.

Right?! It's a brutal trade-off. And you've been making it for years without even realizing it.

The Lie You've Been Told About Being An Empath

There's this idea floating around that being an empath means you're supposed to absorb everyone's pain and transmute it into love. That you're some kind of emotional garbage disposal for the people around you. Let me be very clear about something: that's not empathy. That's codependency in a spiritual costume.

Real empathy doesn't require you to take on someone else's pain. Real empathy is the ability to understand what someone is feeling without losing yourself in it. You can feel for someone without feeling with them. You can hold space for their struggle without jumping into the pit with them.

Think of it this way. If your friend is drowning, you don't jump in and drown with them. You throw them a rope from the shore. You stay on solid ground so you have the strength to pull them out. But if you're in the water too, you're both going down.

That's what fixing is. It's jumping in the water. It's thinking that if you just feel their pain enough, you can somehow make it stop. But you can't. You can only drown together.

How To Tell The Difference Between Helping And Fixing

This is where it gets tricky. Because helping is good. Helping is healthy. We're supposed to help each other. That's part of being human. But fixing is different. Here's how you can tell them apart:

Helping: You offer support and then step back. You trust the other person to make their own choices. You don't need them to take your advice. You're okay if they don't change.

Fixing: You need them to change. You feel anxious if they don't take your advice. You keep pushing, suggesting, reminding, and rescuing. You feel responsible for their outcomes.

Helping: You listen without trying to solve. You let them vent without jumping in with solutions. You ask, "Do you want advice or do you want me to just listen?"

Fixing: You can't stand to see them struggle. You jump in with solutions before they even ask. You feel like it's your job to make them feel better.

Helping: You have clear boundaries. You know where you end and they begin. You can say no without guilt.

Fixing: Your boundaries are a suggestion at best. You say yes when you mean no. You feel guilty when you can't help.

See the difference? Helping comes from a place of fullness. Fixing comes from a place of emptiness - you're trying to fill yourself up by being needed.

One of the best resources I've found for understanding this dynamic is Melody Beattie's classic book Codependent No More. It's not specifically about empaths, but it might as well be. She breaks down exactly why we get hooked into fixing and how to get out. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Three Questions That Will Change Everything

I want to give you something practical. Something you can use the next time you feel that urge to fix someone. Because the urge isn't going away overnight. You've been doing this for years. It's wired into your nervous system. But you can rewire it.

The next time you feel yourself leaning into fixer mode, stop. Take a breath. And ask yourself these three questions:

1. Did they ask for my help?
This is the most important question. If the answer is no, you're fixing. Period. Even if you can see exactly what they need. Even if they're making a huge mistake. Even if you know the solution. If they didn't ask, it's not yours to give.

2. Am I trying to control the outcome?
Be honest with yourself here. Are you offering help because you genuinely want to support them, or because you can't stand the anxiety of not knowing what will happen? Fixing is almost always about controlling the situation so you can feel safe. But their life isn't yours to control.

3. What would happen if I did nothing?
This is the scary one. What if you just... let them figure it out? What if you trusted that they're capable adults who can handle their own lives? What if you let them fail? Because here's the truth: failure is how people learn. When you fix them, you steal their learning. You steal their growth. You keep them small.

I know this is hard. I know it feels selfish. But it's not. It's the most loving thing you can do - for them and for yourself.

What To Do With The Energy You're Not Using To Fix

Here's the thing about stopping the fixing. You're going to have all this energy that used to go into other people's problems. Energy that's been tied up in anxiety, worry, and rescuing. And if you don't have something to put that energy into, you'll go right back to fixing. It's like an addiction. You need a replacement behavior.

So what do you do with all that freed-up energy? You turn it back on yourself. I know, I know. That sounds selfish. But it's not. It's necessary.

Start with these three things:

1. Feel your own feelings.
You've been so busy feeling other people's feelings that you've probably lost touch with your own. Spend time each day checking in with yourself. What are you feeling right now? Not what are you feeling about your friend's divorce or your sister's job loss. What are you feeling? Sad? Angry? Lonely? Excited? Let yourself feel it without trying to fix it.

2. Follow your own curiosity.
What do you actually care about? What lights you up when nobody's watching? What did you love before you became everyone's emotional support system? Start doing those things again. Even for five minutes a day.

3. Practice receiving.
This is going to be the hardest one. You're so used to being the giver that receiving probably feels uncomfortable. Maybe even wrong. But you have to learn to let people give to you. Let someone buy you coffee. Let someone listen to you complain. Let someone help you without trying to pay them back. Just receive.

If you want to go deeper into this work, I highly recommend Brené Brown's book The Gifts of Imperfection. She talks about the difference between connection and fitting in, and why we're so afraid to let people see the real us. See on Amazon (paid link)

What Happens When You Stop Fixing

I want to be honest with you about what happens when you stop fixing. It's not all sunshine and roses. Some people are going to be pissed at you. The people who relied on your fixing are going to feel abandoned. They're going to push your buttons. They're going to try to pull you back in.

And you're going to feel guilty. You're going to feel like a bad person. You're going to want to go back to the old way because it's familiar and it makes you feel needed.

But here's what else happens:

You get your life back. All that energy you were pouring into other people's problems? It's yours again. You can use it for your own growth, your own dreams, your own healing.

Your relationships get real. The people who stick around when you stop fixing? Those are the real ones. They don't need you to save them. They just want to be with you. And the relationships that fall away? They were based on need, not love.

You start to trust yourself. When you stop looking outside yourself for validation through fixing, you have to start looking inside. And when you do, you find that you actually know what you need. You actually have wisdom. You've just been drowning it out with everyone else's noise.

Your intuition gets louder. This is the gift. When you stop focusing on everyone else's problems, your own inner voice starts to speak. And it's been trying to get your attention for a long time. It has things to tell you. Things about your life, your purpose, your path.

For more on this, I love Sonia Choquette's work. Her book The Answer Is Simple is a great guide to reconnecting with your intuition after years of ignoring it. See on Amazon (paid link)

But What If They Really Need Me?

I hear this one a lot. "But Kalesh, what if it's a real emergency? What if my friend is in crisis? What if my partner is struggling with depression? Am I supposed to just sit there and do nothing?"

No. Of course not. There are real emergencies. There are times when someone genuinely needs support. But here's the thing you need to understand: you can support someone without fixing them.

Supporting someone looks like this:

  • You listen without trying to solve.
  • You ask what they need instead of assuming you know.
  • You offer resources (therapists, hotlines, books) instead of being the resource.
  • You check in without taking over.
  • You trust them to know what's best for their own life.

Fixing someone looks like this:

  • You take over because you think you know better.
  • You give unsolicited advice.
  • You feel responsible for their choices.
  • You get anxious when they don't do what you suggest.
  • You lose yourself in their problems.

See the difference? You can be there for someone without being their savior. You can love them without losing yourself. You can hold space without carrying their weight.

And if you're not sure whether you're supporting or fixing, go back to the three questions. Did they ask? Are you trying to control the outcome? What would happen if you did nothing?

The Practice of Not Fixing

This isn't a one-time decision. It's a practice. You're going to slip. You're going to catch yourself in fixer mode. You're going to feel the urge to jump in and save someone. That's okay. That's normal. You're not bad for having the urge. You're human.

Here's what the practice looks like:

Morning check-in: Before you interact with anyone, check in with yourself. What are you feeling? What do you need? Fill your own cup first. You can't pour from an empty one.

Set an intention: Say to yourself, "Today I will support without fixing. I will listen without solving. I will trust others to handle their own lives."

Pause before responding: When someone comes to you with a problem, take a breath before you respond. Ask yourself: Am I about to fix or support?

Ask permission: "Do you want me to just listen, or do you want my input?" This one question will change everything. It puts the power back in their hands.

End of day reflection: Did I fix anyone today? How did it feel? What could I have done differently? Be honest. No judgment. Just awareness.

I also recommend keeping a journal for this work. Something like The Empath's Empowerment Journal by Cyndi Dale can be really helpful for tracking your progress and noticing patterns. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Most Important Person To Stop Fixing

I'm going to leave you with something that might sting a little. The person you've been trying to fix the most isn't your friend, your partner, or your parent. It's you.

You've been trying to fix yourself your whole life. You've been trying to make yourself smaller, quieter, more acceptable. You've been trying to fix your sensitivity, your intensity, your ability to feel everything. You've been trying to fix the part of you that knows things you shouldn't know and feels things you shouldn't feel.

But here's the truth: you're not broken. You were never broken. You were just told you were broken by a world that doesn't understand people like you. A world that's afraid of depth. A world that wants everyone to stay on the surface where it's safe.

You don't need to be fixed. You need to be seen. You need to be accepted. You need to be loved - by yourself, first and foremost.

So here's your new job: stop fixing yourself. Start accepting yourself. Start trusting yourself. Start loving yourself - not for who you could be if you were "better," but for who you are right now, in this moment, with all your messy, beautiful, overwhelming sensitivity.

And when you do that? When you stop fixing yourself? You'll naturally stop fixing everyone else. Because you'll know, deep in your bones, that nobody needs fixing. We all just need to
